title: Octopus in Red Wine
subtitle: Greek slow-braised octopus with wine and spices
excerpt: Octopus braised slowly in red wine until tender is one of the great Greek seafood dishes. The octopus softens over 45 to 60 minutes in a wine and spice braise, turning from rubbery to silky. The wine sauce concentrates around it and makes a good sauce for the serving.
sourceNotes: Htapodi krasato, octopus in wine, is a classic of Greek coastal cooking. The technique of slow-braising octopus in wine with bay leaves and allspice is common across Greek islands and coastal towns. The key is the long, low cooking time, which converts the tough muscle of the octopus.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The braised octopus improves overnight. Reheat gently in the sauce.

glossaryTerms.definition: A slow-cooking method where food is cooked in a small amount of liquid in a covered pot. The long, gentle heat breaks down tough proteins into something very tender.

content.text: in red wine is a Greek coastal dish that turns the octopus very tender and gives the wine a deep, savoury quality. The method is hands-off: the octopus needs no water added and cooks in its own juices and the wine. It takes about an hour and the result is very different from the rubbery octopus of quick cooking.
Ask your fishmonger to clean the octopus; most already come cleaned. If you want to char it afterwards, grill the tentacles briefly over high heat once braised.

items.prepNote: cleaned, tentacles separated from body and cut into pieces

content.text: Heat {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a large casserole over high heat. Add the octopus pieces and cook for 3 to 4 minutes, stirring, until they change colour and give up some liquid. The octopus releases a lot of water; let most of it evaporate.
Reduce the heat to medium. Add the sliced onion and garlic. Stir for 2 minutes.

Pour in {{red-wine}} of red wine. Add the bay leaves, allspice berries, black peppercorns, and tomato purée if using. Stir to combine. Add no water; the octopus will release enough liquid on its own. Cover tightly and simmer over very low heat for 45 to 60 minutes until the octopus is very tender when tested with a sharp knife.

Remove the lid and increase the heat to medium for the last 10 minutes so the sauce reduces to a thick glaze around the octopus. Remove the bay leaves and whole spices. Scatter with parsley and serve with bread or over orzo.

items.symptom: The octopus is still tough after 45 minutes
items.cause: Octopus was very large, or heat was not consistent
items.fix: Replace the lid and continue cooking for another 15 to 20 minutes. Large octopus can take up to 90 minutes. Test with a knife; it should go through with no resistance.

items.symptom: The sauce is too thin after reducing
items.fix: Remove the octopus and reduce the sauce briskly for 5 minutes, then return the octopus to glaze in it.

items.symptom: The octopus is rubbery and difficult to chew
items.cause: Undercooked; the texture is at its worst at 20 to 30 minutes
items.fix: Cook for longer. Octopus goes through a rubbery phase before becoming tender. The texture softens completely with sufficient time.

content.text: Octopus in wine is a signature of Greek coastal and island cooking. It appears on taverna menus throughout the Aegean and Ionian, sometimes with pasta (pasta krasata), sometimes simply with bread. The dish keeps well and is very good reheated the next day when the wine sauce has had time to deepen.
